turns.user_message: Honestly, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. I've been smoking for years, and I know I need to quit, but it's just so hard. I've tried before, and I always end up going back to it. I'm worried that if I quit, my depression and anxiety will get worse.
step2_rag_chunks: Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Honestly, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. I've been smoking for years, and I know I need to quit, but it's just so hard. I've tried before, and I always end up going back to it. I'm worried that if I quit, my depression and anxiety will get worse.

Therapist: I understand your concerns, Emily. It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge the challenges you're facing. Can you tell me more about what makes you want to quit smoking now? What's different this time?

Patient: I guess I'm just tired of feeling like a slave to cigarettes. I hate the way they make me feel, and I'm worried about the long-term health effects. But at the same time, I'm scared to quit because I don't know how I'll cope with my emotions without them. And to be honest, I'm also worried about gaining weight. I've struggled with that in the past, and it just feels like another thing to deal with.

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Honestly, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. I've been smoking for years, and I know I need to quit, but it's just so hard. I've tried before, and I always end up going back to it. I'm worried that if I quit, my depression and anxiety will get worse.

Therapist: I understand your concerns, Emily. It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge the challenges you're facing. Can you tell me more about what makes you want to quit smoking now? What's different this time?

Patient: I guess I'm just tired of feeling like a slave to cigarettes. I hate the way they make me feel, and I'm worried about the long-term health effects. But at the same time, I'm scared to quit because I don't know how I'll cope with my emotions without them. And to be honest, I'm also worried about gaining weight. I've struggled with that in the past, and it just feels like another thing to deal with.

Therapist: I see. It sounds like you're feeling a bit stuck between your desire to quit and your fears about what might happen if you do. That's completely normal. Can you tell me more about how you've been managing your depression and anxiety? How do you think quitting smoking might affect those conditions?

Patient: Well, I've been seeing a therapist and taking medication, which has been helping. But I'm worried that quitting smoking will just add another stress to my life, and I don't know if I can handle it. And to be honest, I've been using marijuana sometimes to relax, and I know that's not good for me either. I just feel like I'm stuck in this cycle of addiction.
